opening_excerpt:
Your friend hasn't texted back in an hour. Your jaw tightens and your mind spins through reasons why.

It's 11pm. You finished your homework an hour ago. Your phone is face-down on the desk. You know you should sleep. But there's a pull — what if someone replied, what if something happened, what if the group chat moved on without you. You flip it over. Nothing urgent. But now you're in it.

The checking becomes the thing. Not the content — the possibility inside the checking. Each notification reinforces the spiral: there might be something, so you look. Nothing, so you look again. The spiral is what the anxiety built.

That's the notification spiral. It will come up throughout this book — not because it's the only thing happening, but because it tends to be underneath most of what we're going to look at.

During class discussion, your teacher asks your opinion about the reading. Everyone turns to look at you, waiting for words to emerge. Your mouth opens but nothing comes out for several seconds. The silence stretches longer and becomes more heavy and strange. You finally say something quiet that nobody can hear clearly. Your face burns hot and your ears turn dark red. The teacher repeats your answer to the whole class kindly. You slide down in your seat and study your desk intensely.

That pause is doing more than slowing you down. It is showing you how the pattern enters, which means the chapter can name it before it hardens.

The comparison engine runs on incomplete data. It takes someone else's visible output and measures it against your full interior experience. The comparison always loses because it is rigged — you see their best against your worst. The notification spiral is automatic and relentless. Understanding it does not stop it. But it lets you see the score as inaccurate.

The point is that the notification spiral treats every decision like a permanent threat.
